text: By 2030, many experts predict we will have artificial general intelligence (AGI)—machines that can perform any intellectual task a human can. Unlike today’s narrow AI, this "general AI" would learn and adapt across different fields. The potential benefits are huge: AGI could accelerate medical discoveries, solve climate problems, or automate tedious jobs, freeing us for creative work. However, risks are serious too. An uncontrolled AGI might make unfair decisions or be used for harmful purposes. Some scientists think AGI could arrive within a few decades, others say centuries. Preparing for both its promises and dangers is crucial for our future.
